coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots for application of alleviating amyloid-β mediated neurotoxicity
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Haolin
Zhang, Yufei
Ding, Jianwei
Wu, Ting
Cai, Shuangfei
Zhang, Wei
Cai, Rong
Chen, Chunying
Yang, Rong
coredata.dc:description: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) has been an intractable neurodegenerative disease among the elderly in the worldwide. One of the important pathogenic factors in AD is the aggregation of amyloid-β 1-42 (Aβ42) in AD patients’ brain. Inhibition of Aβ peptide aggregation is considered as one of the effective approaches against AD. Herein, a pulsed laser ablation (PLA) method is used to fabricate ultra-small carbon quantum dots (C-QDs) with uniform size. The reduced Aβ42 aggregation by the C-QDs was confirmed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), thioflavin T assay and circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy. The results of cell viability experiments showed that the presence of the C-QDs could significantly reduce the cytotoxicity of Aβ42. Furthermore, in vivo studies demonstrate that C-QDs can decrease Aβ42 deposits and promote the biological activity of an AD model of Caenorhabditis elegans CL2006. This work demonstrates the viability of using ultrasmall C-QDs to inhibit amyloid-β aggregation and alleviate amyloid-β mediated neurotoxicity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Pulsed laser ablation method
Alzheimer’s disease
Aβ42 aggregation
Aβ42 inhibitors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of C-QDs on the inhibition of Aβ monomer aggregation assay
Reduced Aβ42 toxicity by C-QDs in vitro and in vivo

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of fabrication of C-QDs and their inhibition effects on Aβ42 aggregation.
(a) The TEM image and corresponding SAED image (inset) of C-QDs; (b) The HRTEM image of C-QDs; (c) the size distribution of C-QDs; (d) the Raman spectrum of C-QDs.
(a) The FTIR spectrum of C-QDs; (b)The UV–vis spectrum of C-QDs.

(a) ThT fluorescence changes of Aβ42 with different concentrations of C-QDs; (b) CD spectra of Aβ42 with different concentrations of C-QDs; (c) TEM images of Aβ42 alone, and with different concentrations of C-QDs: (d)10 μg/mL, (e) 20 μg/mL, (f) 40 μg/mL.
(a) Tyrosine fluorescence changes of Aβ42 in the presence of different concentrations of C-QDs; (b) ANS fluorescence for Aβ42 alone or incubated with different concentration of C-QDs.
(a) SH-SY5Y cell viability in the presence of different concentrations of C-QDs; (b) SH-SY5Y cell viability in the presence of Aβ42 alone, and in the presence of Aβ42 and 5 μg/mL C-QDs.

C-QDs alleviated Aβ-induced toxicity of CL 2006 nematodes. (a)–(c) Representative ThS-staining images of Aβ deposits which were marked with arrows. (a) N2 strains; (b) CL2006 cultured on NGM alone (c) CL2006 cultured on NGM containing C-QDs (10 μg/mL). Scale bars are 20 µm.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots for application of alleviating amyloid-β mediated neurotoxicity

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory for Biomedical Effects of Nanomaterials and Nanosafety, Center of Materials Science and Optoelectronics Engineering, National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) has been an intractable neurodegenerative disease among the elderly in the worldwide. One of the important pathogenic factors in AD is the aggregation of amyloid-β 1-42 (Aβ42) in AD patients’ brain. Inhibition of Aβ peptide aggregation is considered as one of the effective approaches against AD. Herein, a pulsed laser ablation (PLA) method is used to fabricate ultra-small carbon quantum dots (C-QDs) with uniform size. The reduced Aβ42 aggregation by the C-QDs was confirmed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), thioflavin T assay and circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy. The results of cell viability experiments showed that the presence of the C-QDs could significantly reduce the cytotoxicity of Aβ42. Furthermore, in vivo studies demonstrate that C-QDs can decrease Aβ42 deposits and promote the biological activity of an AD model of Caenorhabditis elegans CL2006. This work demonstrates the viability of using ultrasmall C-QDs to inhibit amyloid-β aggregation and alleviate amyloid-β mediated neurotoxicity.

ce:section.ce:para: As a cureless neurodegenerative disease, Alzheimer’s disease (AD) has persecuted the elderly more than 50 million in the world [1,2]. Extracellular amyloid plaques and intracellular neurofibrillary tangles are prevalently considered as the mainspring for senile dementia, which mainly include deposits of amyloid-β (Aβ) peptides [3–5]. The misfolding of Aβ peptides into fibrils was one of the most important pathogenic factors of AD [6–8]. Thus, the inhibition of the Aβ aggregation is one of the effective means to relief the symptom of AD [9–11].

To date, various of aggregation inhibitors have been reported including small molecules, antibodies and peptides, which can interact with Aβ peptides through electrostatic, hydrogen bonding (H-bonding), π–π stacking and hydrophobic interactions [12–15]. In the past few years, inorganic nanomaterials, such as graphene oxides, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), MoO3 dots, Au nanoparticles, and MoS2- nanosheets, etc, were found to have inhibition effects on Aβ aggregation [16–22]. Carbon quantum dots (C-QDs) have been attracted much attention in bio-applications due to their unique properties, biocompatibility and versatile functionalities [23–27]. For example, cancer therapy [28,29], vivo imaging [30], and biosensing [31].

In the last decades, various methods were employed to fabricated C-QDs, including hydrothermal method, acid reagent engineering method, solid-phase microwave-assisted and pulsed laser irradiation method [32–36]. Pulsed laser ablation (PLA) method attracted much attention due to its unique feature. Comparing with other methods, the PLA in liquid has some advantages, such as relatively convenient procedure and less chemical remnants [37–42]. Herein, the PLA method was used to fabricated C-QDs. The ultra-small carbon dots without any surfactant can be obtained.

The prepared C-QDs were characterized with transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV–vis spectroscopy, FTIR spectrum, Raman spectrum and photoluminescence spectrum. The aggregation of Aβ42 peptides in the presence and absence of the C-QDs was studied by Thioflavin T fluorescence and TEM. The second structures of Aβ42 in the presence and absence of C-QDs were studied by circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy. Tyrosine fluorescence and ANS (8-anilino-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid) fluorescence were also used to investigate the possible mechanism for the interactions between the C-QDs and Aβ42 peptides. The cell viability experiments showed that the C-QDs could significantly reduce the cytotoxicity of Aβ42. Furthermore, in vivo studies demonstrate that C-QDs can decrease Aβ42 deposits and promote the biological activity of an AD model of Caenorhabditis elegans CL2006.

β-Amyloid peptides (Aβ42, purity > 98%) were obtained from Shanghai Science Peptide Biological Technology Co. Ltd, China. Graphite were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co, Ltd. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was obtained from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. ANS fluorescence probe was obtained from Tokyo Chemical Industry. Nematodes and NGM were bought from SunyBiotech (Fujian, China). All reagents and chemicals were purchased commercially and without further purification if not otherwise mentioned, and Ultrapure Milli-Q water (18.25 MW) was used in all experiments.

The carbon quantum dots (C-QDs) were fabricated by the pulsed laser ablation method. Firstly, the graphite target pellet was fabricated by graphite through a desk-top powder presser, which was subsequently polished and cleaned by water. Then the graphite target pellet was put into the bottom of a quartz tube with 4 mL water or ethanol. A Pulsed Nd: YAG nanosecond laser with the wavelength of 532 nm was used to ablate the graphite target pellet. The frequency and pulse energy of the laser and are 10 Hz and 120 mJ. The water-cooling system maintains the temperature at about 25 ℃ to support the normal running of the system. In a typical procedure, the graphite pellet target in 4 mL ethanol was ablated by laser for 10 min through a focus lens. After that, the graphite pellet was taken out, the obtained solution was ablated by the laser for another 10 min without the focus lens. The laser energy was 120 mJ during the whole fabricated process. Then the C-QDs dispersed in yellow supernatant was obtained by centrifugation and redispersed into water after the low-pressure rotary evaporation.

The morphology and size of C-QDs were studied by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20 U-TWIN, FEI company, America). The Raman spectrum of materials was represented by a confocal laser with an excitation wavelength of 514 nm (laser Raman spectrometer, Renishaw inVia plus, Renishaw company, Britain). The UV–vis and FTIR absorption spectra of materials were studied by a multimode plate reader (Envision, PerkinElmer, America) and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, spotlight 200i, PerkinElmer, America). A multifunctional fluorescence spectrometer (FluoroMax+, Horiba, America) was used to measure the fluorescence spectroscopy of the fabricated materials.

Aβ42 was dissolved in hexafluorisopropanol (HFIP) solution (1 mg/mL) and stored at −20 °C as the stock solution. Before use, the HFIP was removed completely by a gentle stream of nitrogen. and the peptides were put in vacuum for 24 h. After being put in vacuum for 24 h, The peptides were redissolved using Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) for future use.

The fibrillation of the Aβ42 was studied by Thioflavin T (ThT) fluorescence experiment. Typically, Aβ42 alone or co-incubated with C-QDs in PBS and ThT solution were stirred at 37 ℃ for 72 h. The fluorescence of ThT (485 nm) was measured by the multimode plate reader at the excitation of 450 nm at different time points until 72 h.

Tyrosine and ANS fluorescence spectra were used to study the interaction between Aβ42 and C-QDs. Firstly, Aβ42 solution was incubated alone or with different concentration of C-QDs in PBS for 72 h. For tyrosine fluorescence assay, the fluorescence emission intensity of the sample solution between 295 and 450 nm was measured at the excitation wavelength of 280 nm. For ANS fluorescence, after mixing 200 μL sample solution with 6 μL ANS-Na (10 mM) solution, the ANS fluorescence between 420 nm and 650 nm was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 400 nm.

Circular dichroism spectrometer (J-1500, Jasco, Japan) was used to determine the secondary structure of Aβ42. Aβ42 was pre-incubated alone or with C-QDs at 37 ℃ for 72 h. The CD spectra between 185 nm and 260 nm were collected.

The morphology of Aβ42 aggregation was characterized by TEM. 10 μL sample solution was added on 200 mesh carbon-coated copper grids after incubation for 72 h. Then the sample was negatively stained with 2% phosphotungstic acid for 2 min. Tecnai G2 F20 U-TWIN electron microscope was operated at 200 kV.

SH-SY5Y cells were cultured in 85% 1640 basal medium and 15% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS) for 3 days. Then, 100 μL basal medium contained 10,000 cells was mixed with C-QDs and Aβ42 solution and was incubated for 24 h continuously. 10 μL CCK-8 solution was added into the above solution and the absorbance at 450 nm was measured after culturing for 2–3 h.

C. elegans strains (N2 normal strain and the transgenic CL2006 strain) were cultured with E. coli (OP50) on nematode growth medium (NGM) at 20 ℃. In the next experiment, CL2006 nematodes were incubated with C-QDs (10 μg/mL) on NGM for 24 h. For fluorescence staining of Aβ deposits, worms were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 24 h. After rinsed by PBS, β-mercaptoethanol and Tris-HCl (pH 7.4) containing 1%TritonX-100 were add and the solution incubated for another 24 h at 37 ℃. Then ThS was used to stain the worms after washing by PBS. Finally, the nematodes were observed by a laser confocal microscope after resuspending the nematodes in M9 buffer.

The schematic illustration of fabrication of C-QDs and their inhibition effects on Aβ42 aggregation is shown in Fig. 1. The C-QDs were prepared by pulsed laser ablation (PLA) method and the morphology and size can be controlled by the laser energy and ablation time. To investigate the influence of the PLA parameters, we fabricated the material under different conditions in the water firstly. Fig. S1 shows the TEM images of C-QDs fabricated by different laser energy for 5 min. We can see that the increase of laser energy can increase the yield of nanomaterials. The influence of ablation time was shown in Fig. S2, the carbon target was ablated using laser energy of 200 mJ for 20, 40 and 60 min, respectively. As a result, the size of the material was about 2–4 nm and there was no obvious diversity between different ablation time of 40 and 60 min. We also fabricated the C-QDs in ethanol and collected through the low-pressure rotary evaporation. As shown in Fig. S3, when the laser energy is less than 80 mJ, a layered graphite structure appeared. As the laser energy increased, the layered structure became smaller and was replaced by nanoparticles.

To obtain C-QDs with good dispersibility and homogeneous size, two-step PLA method in ethanol was carried out. The first irradiation of the target is to obtain micro/nanoparticles, while the second ablation is to reduce the nanoparticle size and homogenise the size dispersion [39–42]. As shown in Fig. 2a and Fig. S4, the fabricated C-QDs were uniform after the second ablation, whose average size was about 2.2 ± 0.6 nm (Fig. 2c). The typical high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of C-QDs was shown in Fig. 2b, lattice fringes of 0.21 nm and 0.32 nm were attributed to the spacing of (100) and (002) crystal plane of the graphite structure (PDF-#41-1487), which was consistent with the previous reports [38]. The SAED result (inset Fig. 2a) also demonstrated the graphite structure of the materials. The Raman spectrum was further used to characterize the fabricated carbon material. The D band at 1352 cm−1 and G band at 1578 cm−1 in Fig. 2d were determined as the characteristic peaks of carbon nanocrystals such as amorphous carbon, carbon quantum dots and graphene quantum dots. The peak at 2701 cm−1 was attributed to the 2D band, whose symmetry decreases with the increasing layers of graphene. For carbon materials, the enhanced D band is corresponding to the imperfect of sp2 hybridization, which represents the defects and disorders of carbon nanocrystals. The G band is associated with the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a 2D hexagonal lattice, which will have a blue shift with the increase of graphene layers. The integral areas values of D and G peaks was calculated as 0.34 (Id/Ig), which suggested the high graphitization degree.

The functional groups on the surface of C-QDs were determined by FTIR spectrum. As shown in Fig. 3a, the wavenumber at 2919, 2851 and 1369 cm−1 were ascribed to the C-H stretching vibration. The CC and CO bond showed the characteristic peak at 1557 and 1740 cm−1, which demonstrated the oxygen species on the surface of C-QDs. The appearance of C-H and CO could be due to the reaction between carbon nanoparticles and ethanol. Fig. 3b is the UV–visible spectrum of C-QDs. The result exhibited 5 absorption peaks from 230 nm to 320 nm. The absorption band at 234, 252, 276 nm were mainly ascribed to the (π → π*) transitions of aromatic CC bond and peaks at 297, 315 nm arise from (n → π*) transitions of CO bond, respectively. As reported that C-QDs with lattice spacing of 0.31 nm can generate fluorescence [43]. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the fabricated C-QDs by different excitation wavelengths (λex) from 234 to 400 nm were shown in Fig. S5. The PL intensity and maximal emission wavelengths (λem) were dependent on λex, which was consistent with the literature report [44]. There were no obvious emission peaks under excitation wavelength at λex < 252 nm and the maximized PL intensity was observed at λex = 315 nm. The λem had a red shift with the increase of λex. The emission-dependent phenomenon maybe the oxygenic groups (CO) on the surface of C-QDs, which cause the surface states energy levels between π and π* of CC. Time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy was further used to study the optical behavior of C-QDs (Fig. S6). The photoluminescence decay curve was fitted by three-component exponential method, in which the lifetime of three dominant components were 2.02, 7.07 and 22.21 ns. The average fluorescence lifetime was calculated as 7.41 ns. All results above indicated that C-QDs were fabricated by PLA method successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of C-QDs on the inhibition of Aβ monomer aggregation assay
ce:section.ce:para: ThT was used to evaluate the aggregation of amyloid Aβ42 since it can bind to β-folded structure of Aβ42 fibrils, which have the maximal emission intensity at 485 nm under the excitation wavelength at 450 nm. The reduced fluorescence intensity in the experiment suggested the reduced aggregation of Aβ42. Fig. 4a showed the ThT fluorescence intensity change over the reaction time of Aβ42 with different concentration of C-QDs. The strongest fluorescence intensity of Aβ42 implied the aggregation of peptides while the greatly reduced intensity was observed with the addition of C-QDs. The inhibition effect was depended on concentrations (10, 20 40 μg/mL) of C-QDs, whose fluorescence intensity were reduced by approximately 28%, 42% and 55%, respectively. The ThT fluorescence signals in the experiments which only C-QDs (40 μg/mL) were present in were very weak. The inhibition aggregation of Aβ42 can also be observed by TEM images shown in Fig. 4c–f. The Aβ42 incubated alone can form a reticular fibril structure. The fibers became rare and shorter after the co-incubation with C-QDs, indicating the inhibitory effect of C-QDs. CD spectra were applied to study the secondary structure of Aβ42 peptides, which exhibited a positive peak at around 195 nm and a negative valley at 217 nm. As incubated with C-QDs, the intensity of the negative peak at 217 nm as expected for β-sheets became weaker. The negative peak reduced gradually with the increase of the concentration of C-QDs, suggesting the ratio of β-sheet structure was much less than that of Aβ42 alone and secondary structures became random coils or amorphous aggregates.

To investigate the interaction between C-QDs and Aβ42, the tyrosine fluorescence spectra were carried out subsequently. Tyrosine is located at the 10th site in the sequence of Aβ42 which has self-fluorescence at the excitation wavelength of 280 nm. Fig. 5a showed the tyrosine spectra of Aβ42 alone and that co-incubated with C-QDs. The Tyr fluorescence intensity decreased obviously after the addition of C-QDs compared with Aβ42 alone. With the increase of the concentration (10, 20, 40 μg/mL) of C-QDs, the fluorescence intensity of tyrosine decreased by about 18%, 28% and 45%, respectively. Besides, the fluorescence peak had a red shifted from 327 nm to 332 nm, indicating that C-QDs may interact with Aβ42, which can quench the fluorescence of tyrosine. We also measured the fluorescence spectrum of C-QDs and tyrosine fluorescence of Aβ42 at the excitation wavelength of 280 nm for comparison. The results shown in Fig. S7 indicated that the C-QDs had much low intensity and would have little influence on tyrosine fluorescence experiments. The ANS fluorescence spectrum was further applied to study the interaction between C-QDs and Aβ42. As a kind of sensitive hydrophobic probe, ANS-Na can bind to the hydrophobic site of Aβ42 which has the fluorescence at 420–650 nm under the excitation wavelength at 400 nm. As displayed in Fig. 5b, it performed a strong fluorescence signal when the Aβ42 was incubated with ANS probe, suggesting that there existed the hydrophobic sites in the Aβ42 peptide. The fluorescence intensity decreased with the increased concentration of C-QDs. It indicated that C-QDs may interact with the hydrophobic sites of Aβ42 peptides. The ANS fluorescence was very weak when only C-QDs presented.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reduced Aβ42 toxicity by C-QDs in vitro and in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The toxicity of Aβ42 peptide were evaluated by the viability of human neuroblastoma cells (SH-SY5Y) with and without C-QDs. First, we investigated cytotoxicity of C-QDs to the cells. Fig. 6a is the cell viability in the presence of different concentrations of C-QDs. As demonstrated, the survival rate of SY5Y cells decreased with the increase of the concentration of C-QDs after incubation for 24 h. At low concentration, C-QDs did not show obvious toxicity. 5 μg/mL C-QDs was used to further study their modulation effects on Aβ42 cytotoxicity to SH-SY5Y cells. As shown in Fig. 6b, the Aβ42 exhibited a noticeable cell toxicity. While cell viability increased obviously when the C-QDs were added. When the concentration of Aβ42 was 40 μM, the cell viability could raise to 55.3% from 34% after the addition of C-QDs, indicating that C-QDs could reduce the toxicity of Aβ42 peptides to SH-SY5Y cells.

The transgenic strain C. elegans CL2006 is usually used as the AD study model in vivo, which comprises Aβ peptide expressed in muscle cells. To study whether C-QDs can reduce the aggregation of Aβ, thioflavin S (ThS) staining assay was carried out to determine the amyloid plaques of nematodes. The ThS fluorescence images in different treatments were shown in Fig. 7. As displayed in Fig. 7a, the wild-type N2 worms showed the uniform fluorescence intensity, indicating the negligible Aβ plaques. Comparing with that of N2 strain, the aggregation of Aβ peptides increased apparently in the untreated CL2006 strains (Fig. 7b) and most nematodes became sluggish even dead in this experiment. However, Aβ deposits decreased after fed with C-QDs as demonstrated in Fig. 7c. And C-QDs could alleviate Aβ-caused paralysis of C. elegans CL2006 and extend their life span.

In summary, ultra-small C-QDs with the uniform morphology have been prepared by PLA method. ThT fluorescence and TEM experimental results demonstrated the efficient inhibition of Aβ42 aggregation by C-QDs. Moreover, quenching of tyrosine and ANS fluorescence of the Aβ42 solutions with C-QDs indicated an interaction between the C-QDs and Aβ42 peptides. The cell viability experiments revealed that the presence of the C-QDs could greatly reduce the cytotoxicity of Aβ42. Furthermore, the decrease of Aβ42 deposits and increase biological activity of an AD model of C elegans CL2006 by C-QDs was also observed. In addition, The PLA method demonstrated here can be extended to fabricate other nanodots which can be used in various biomedical applications. We should mention that the surface of C-QDs prepared by PLA can be further modified by different molecules and improve its stability and biocompatibility [45]. The self-fluorescent C-QDs also provide a potential strategy to mark and detect Aβ42 peptides in the future, which can prospectively realize the integration of diagnosis and treatment for AD. Our work demonstrated the C-QDs is one of the promising candidates for inhibiting the aggregation of Aβ peptides to alleviate AD.

Haolin Li: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yufei Zhang: Visualization, Investigation,Validation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Jianwei Ding: Investigation, Formal analysis. Ting Wu: Resources, Investigation. Shuangfei Cai: Validation, Formal analysis. Wei Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Rong Cai: Validation, Funding acquisition. Chunying Chen: Conceptualization, Validation, Project administration. Rong Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Key Research and Development Program from the Ministry of Science and Technology of China (2021YFE0112600; 2017YFA0303400), the Strategic Priority Research Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (XDB36000000), NSFC-RGC (11861161002) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (12174032, 11774036).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2022.112373.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
